sentences.text: And I found out my brother survive.
And he came home.
And he lives with three other Jewish boys.
There are four of them, together, in a one, big apartment complex.
It used to belong to a very rich Jewish millionaire in town.
And they were afraid to go to our own house, because it was white-- quite very bad and antisemitic.
And they-- they sleep together in one apartment, the one room.
So I walked there.
And I-- when I got there, it was still early in the morning.
It could have-- no more than 7:00 or 7 o'clock in the morning.
And everybody was sleeping.
So I knock on the doors, and they were afraid to open the doors.
I-- I-- they started asking, who is there?
Who is there?
In Russian, [RUSSIAN].
I says, this is Harry Spitz, Harry Spitz from [?
Munkacs. ?] I-- I understand my brother is here, somewhere, Arthur Spitz.
So, they couldn't believe me at first.
They says, who-- who are you?
Harry Spitz.
They says, yeah, yeah, Harry Spitz.
Yeah, yeah.
Who-- how-- what-- what was your mother's name, they started asking me.
So, because they were afraid.
And may-- maybe it's something, a setup.
I said, no, my mother was Julia Spitz.
Oh, [SOBBING] they started [MOAN] crying and opened the door and let me in.
And they-- we had, right away, something to eat, like breakfast and whatever we could eat, whatever they had.
And we were celebrating that he is survived and I'm survived.
And we started drinking and talking, whatever happened to the families?
So we couldn't find out in the beginning.
All we knew, that they were taken away in cattle cars to Auschwitz.
And we didn't know if anybody survived or nobody survived.
We didn't know what happened to them.
So, but it came out every-- once a week, it came out, a Red Cross paper, which was issued by the Red Cross.
And it used to be alphabetically, under sequence-- alphabetically, names and people's names and addresses-- who survived and from which town they come and where they are now.
So, sure enough, I found Julia Spitz [SOBBING] and-- and Livia Spitz and [?
Gretchen ?] Spitz survived in Bergen-Belsen.
[SOBBING] And they got taken to Sweden, by the Swedish Red Cross, to a sanatorium.
They were very sick.
[SOBBING] So I found out that my mother survived and my two sisters.
[CRYING] Excuse me.
And, [SOBBING] right away, I tried to organize to see if I could go to Sweden and visit them and try to help them or do what I-- whatever I can do.
So it wasn't that easy, but, later on, I-- I got somehow together a-- a passport which they would let me go to Sweden.
Your brother went with you?
No.
We didn't go to Sweden yet, because we didn't have the-- the sponsor, anything.
You know, everything had to--
So, and then, the next day, I found another US paper, which says, Joseph Spitz survived-- in the Red Cross paper-- in Theresienstadt.
But he is in a hospital.
And he has-- he has Fleck typhus, so he's all swollen, his legs are swollen and his body from typhus.
He has-- I don't know if you know what typhus is.
So I found out, oh, my father [SOBBING] is alive, too.
[CRYING] So, [SOBBING] so I started asking about the rest of the family.
I said, did you heard about Walter Spitz, my brother?
He says, yeah, we heard that, but I think he got taken to the gas chambers.
So, [SOBBING] and we-- we-- we couldn't-- in the beginning, I just couldn't-- no way to get organized to get to Sweden.
But my-- they said, my-- your father is in Theresienstadt, in the hospital.
But Theresienstadt was Czechoslovakia.
So I could go there with no problem.
So we went to Theresienstadt.
No.
Just me, myself.
My brother was kind of afraid to go.
So I says, if you don't want to go, I'll go.
So, and then, before they-- all that happen, we finally talked in the authorities that they let us back in our own home, where we used to live.
And we knew this, our mother and father buried stuff underground in one of the rooms.
So all of us Jewish boys went there.
And we got shovels from the neighbors.
But the son-of-a-guns, these neighbors, squealed on us to the-- to the authorities, as we digging up [BACKGROUND NOISE] American money, the gold and silver and valuables.
So we got out something.
We tried to get out.
And sure enough, as much as we get out, it wasn't much, but it was more than nothing.
You know, it was worth a nice, few hundred dollars.
The next morning, early in the morning, about 5 o'clock in the morning, they knock on the door.
And there were Russian Banderovcis, Russian soldiers.
And we slept all in one room, on the floors.
And it was a big carpet, a huge carpet, you know, like a throw rug.
And-- and the Russians came in.
He says, you Jews, you dug up a lot of valuables and gold and silver and who knows what else.
And we-- we want those valuables.
So they held machine guns on us, on me and my brother and the other three Jewish boys.
We were four or five boys sleeping in one room.
And-- and they threw everything, even my clothes, my pants, my shirt, everything on the carpet, and the big huge carpet, that throw rug.
And-- and whatever they could find-- they searched the house.
And they took everything, even my last valuable papers.
I had my identification to go to Sweden.
Everything, they threw on that carpet.
And they rolled it up.
And they carried it out to their truck and took off.
So there, I stayed in my underwear.
I didn't have nothing.
And this was already the good times, when we came home.
So, I says, look at what's happening.
It's-- it's just terrible.
I-- we can't stay here.
So I went, in my underwear, to a neighbor, to a Christian house.
And I says, can I borrow a pair of pants?
I got no pants.
They took everything away from us, because those are one, the people who squealed on us.
And sure enough, they gave us a pair of pants.
So big deal.
So gave-- they give me a pair of alt-- old pants.
So we went to the station, to the police station, to report the theft, the robbery, you know?
So oh, sure, sure.
We're going to come out, investigate.
So the Russians came out.
See, they weren't Russian soldiers.
They were Russian bandits, banderovci.
They called them Banderovci.
And they came out to investigate all that, the protocol, the shmotocol.
And-- and-- and they came out two, three times.
And-- and I says, did you find anything?
Do you know?
No, no, we can't.
We don't know who they were.
They were bandits.
And can you-- can we get something back?
No.
We don't have nothing to give you.
We don't-- we don't have it.
So after that, I got so bitter and upset, and my brother, too.
So she let-- we says-- we says, we're gonna leave Munkacs.
We got to get out of here.
So we, with them, we went from the Carpathian back to Czechoslovakia.
And we stayed a couple days in Prague.
And from Prague, I went to Theresienstadt.
And I met my father.
And I had-- whatever I had, I gave it to the doctors, a few-- a few pennies, what, you know, what money was valuable, krones.
I said they should try to help him.
He should get well and should get more medication, what he needed.
So we got-- we got together, me and my brother with my father, and we told my father this, my-- our mother is alive [SOB] with our two sisters.
And they're in Sweden.
And they're in Sweden in a sanatorium.
And we send the letters out there.
And we sent them that we are alive, that Arthur and Harry is alive, and Joseph Spitz is alive.
And he be fine.
He-- he will be cured.
So sure enough, about a couple months later, my-- my-- one of my sisters died in Sweden.
And my mother was very down, because they couldn't cure her anymore.
She was too far gone with tuberculosis from the concentration camps.
But finally, they came to Prague, my mother and one of my sisters who survived, [?
Gretchen. ?] We got all united.
And we decided, we don't go back to our own home no more.
We don't go back to the Munkacs, to Carpathian.
We told our mother what happened to us and-- and how it happened.
And we got robbed, even, from the Russians.
Whatever was leftover, we found.
Everything was taken away.
So we went to a small town, to Liberec, it's called there.
In German, it's called Reichenberg, Liberec.
And we settled down there.
This was in the-- in Czechoslovakia?
That was in Czechoslovakia.
It was Russian-occupied.
It was under Russian occupation, right.
It was communism there.
And my father felt a little better.
And he got a job in-- in a place where they bring in antiques to sell.
And he should charge you how good it is or what it is.
My father was a very clever man.
He was always in-- in the business, you know, buying and selling things.
So he got a job there.
And I got a job, also.
And we all worked.
And we decided we-- we can't stay in Czechoslovakia too long.
We stayed there.
And then, we went back to Germany.
We went back to Germany.
And we wound up in Munich, in the displaced persons camp, where people come, whom have no homes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This place, first I come, the camp's name was-- I don't really remember.
It was a big camp.
It was awful lot of people.
And was all kind people there, not only-- not only men.
There were children and women and whole families.
And-- and, of course, we went in to-- we went in to-- to the authorities.
It was ran by the Americans, already.
It was ran by the Americans.
And we told the people we want to register to emigrate to the United States.
So, and they took our names and birthdays and all the-- the-- the things they had to know about us.
And I told them, I'm a mechanic and so on and so on.
And, sure enough, my brother was-- came out here yet in 1946, because he was young, and he was-- he had a good profession.
And they take those young people, either way.
And they wanted to take me, too.
What was his profession?
He was a-- he was like a-- you know, he was watchmaker, jewelry.
He was very handy.
And-- but he was-- was also a very good businessman.
And-- and so they took him.
And he was already married.
He married right after the war.
His childhood sweetheart was liberated, too, Hede Shmilovicz.
And they came out, in 1946, to the United States.
And they wanted, I should go with them.
I can go, too, because I was young, and I had a plumber and locksmith profession.
But I said, no, I can't go.
Because I don't want to leave my parents by themselves.
Because my mother and father was elderly and sickly, a little bit.
And I says, I can't abandon my parents.
So he says, then you'll-- Harry, if you don't want to go now, I don't know where you can go.
You will have to wait till the quota system comes.
They say, you have to wait for the Czechoslovakian quota that your parents can go.
But I says, my parents have-- my mother and father has sisters and brothers in United States.
So sure enough, they had to investigate.
And we had to get papers to prove that they are really here in this country.
And in 1948, we got our visas to come to the United States.
And I came out, the same time, with my mother and father on the same ship.
And-- and my sister.
My sister was also married.
And she came out even-- also in '46.
Then my brother came out a little earlier.
Because she was young, also, and her husband was very, very, very handy, a very active guy, young man.
So the young people came out first.
And then they took only the older people.
And I-- I waited especially, because I didn't want my-- leave my parents behind.
So I had to wait with the older people.
I came with the older folks.
And you lived in Munich during that time?
In the camp?
I-- we lived in-- in-- no.
From Munich, we went to near Stuttgart.
It's a city called Bensheim.
And my father worked in the kitchen as a cook.
And it was a displaced persons camp there--
--in Bensheim.
And we lived there.
And when we came from Bensheim, we went to, of course, later on, to Bremerhaven.
And we came here.
Yeah, It was a military ship.
And we came to the United States in 1948.
And we settled in New York.
And my mother went back to her old trade.
She started making wigs.
And my father got a job in a-- in a gallery, in a-- in a big gallery where they sell all kind paintings and sculptures and stuff.
And I went to work for a-- in a hotel in the East 10th Avenue in New York, by the river.
It was a big, huge, hotel.
It was strictly for girls, college girls, young girls.
It was-- it was donated, that hotel, by the multimillionaire.
And he-- he sponsored those girls, free.
They used to live there, but they had to go to school, to college.
And they hired me as a handyman and as an elevator man, run up the elevators and fix the windows and locks and whatever I could.
I was a handyman.
And I worked there for about three months.
And I used to sleep.
No-- no man was allowed in the hotel, anyway.
Only in the lobby, they used to be able to come visit if they wanted to visit some-- some student or some girl.
So it was strictly women.
East 10th Avenue hotel, it was a huge hotel.
It was right by the river.
I don't remember anymore the name, but it was a very well-known hotel.
And-- and--
--then they used to deliver their food, groceries.
And a man came in delivering all the time.
And I used to let him in, with the elevator, to take him up or down or whatever, with the food.
And he approached me.
He says, we-- well-- he-- I spoke with a very bad accent.
I couldn't speak good English.
He says, where are you from?
So I started telling him.
And the guy was from Russia, a Russian Jew.
And he says to me, hey, you know what?
This is not the job for you.
You can't-- you can't grow much here.
I says, you want to come to work with me in the grocery store?
And I'll give you a job there, and you can-- but I see what you can do in the grocery store, in the delicatessen.
Sure enough, I quit the job in the hotel.
And I went to work for him.
And I had to find a room with an old lady.
I rented one-- one room.
She had-- an old widow, she had a two-bedroom apartment, three-bedroom apartment.
So she rented me out one room.
And I moved in there.
And I worked in that grocery store for about six months.
And he says to me, Harry, you so accurate and do such a good job, I'm going to make you an assistant manager.
I says, you have here six people besides me.
And they worked for you for eight, nine years already.
And I just started.
How can you make me assistant manager?
I says, I want you, 'cause-- because I see what-- how you do the job.
So he made me assistant manager.
And he gave me the keys to the store.
It was on York Avenue, 1st Street and York, a huge store.
And we had produce.
We had all kind delicatessens.
We-- we had a big kitchen in behind the store, in the back of the store.
We used to make our own roast beefs and Virginia hams and coleslaw and potato salad.
And we used to-- all kind food.
So it was very easy, because I used to eat everything in the-- in the store.
I didn't have to buy food.
And I saved every penny I used to make, every dollar.
And I-- I never spent anything, except what I paid for the room.
It was very cheap.
That time, I think 10-- 10 krone or 10-- $10 for a for a month, for-- just for the room, what I paid.
And I used to, sometimes, take home for the old lady, some food.
And-- and I worked there.
After six months later, I work-- worked there.
Then-- and, after a year, he made me manager, not assistant manager, but manager of the whole store.
He said, you have to sign for the bread man and for the milk man and all of the-- you have to count whatever they bring in.
You're responsible.
Sure enough, I worked there for almost-- a little over two years.
And I saved up a nice few dollars.
I had about $2,000 in the bank, in the savings account.
And used to get, every day, deliveries.
There wasn't a day you shouldn't get deliveries, produce, bread-- bakery, whatever, beer, wine, liquor.
We used to get everything delivered.
Every day was deliveries.
So one day, a-- a bakery man-- it was a Jewish bakery man, Pechter's Bread.
I don't know if you heard-- if it still exists, in Pechter's Bread.
It was kosher, Jewish rye bread and pumpernickels and special Jewish rolls and bagels.
He used to deliver to us.
And he says to me, Harry, I know a store, it's for sale in the Bronx.
And if anybody could make a go of that store, you the guy who could make it go.
That guy is going bankrupt.
It's a Jewish people own it, a nice Jewish couple.
And they're American-born Jewish people.
And they were young people, about in their 30s.
He says, and-- but they just don't know how to run the store.
They're going bankrupt.
They're going mekhula.
I says to him like, listen, Leo, I can't go now to-- I'm still working.
But why don't you come weekends and pick me up?
And we'll-- I'll go and look at the store.
And maybe we can make a deal.
Maybe I can go my own business.
Sure enough, he came Sunday.
And he picked me up.
And he took me out to the the West Bronx.
And it was a little, corner store.
It wasn't much bigger than this two rooms together, that's all.
And-- and-- but it was very neglected.
Everything-- nothing was organized.
And he says, I got about 3-- 3-- $3,000 worth of groceries left in the store.
He says, if you want to buy the store, I'll sell you the store.
But I need cash.
I says, I don't have all that cash.
But he ask, I think, $7,000 for the fixtures and the-- and the-- and the inventory and everything.
I says, I'll tell you what, I have about a little bit less than about half, maybe.
I'll give you all the cash I have.
And I'll make payments to you, every-- not every month, but every week.
Whatever I make profit, I'll give you from the payments from the store.
Sure enough, we made a contract.
And I bought the store.
And I worked there for a whole year without making for myself any wages.
Whatever I made, I put back in the store, and I paid off the debts-- paid them.
After two years, I paid completely all the-- all the debts.
And I owned the store.
And after the third year, I started making more money, more money.
And I started playing even the stock market.
I made so much out of this, I could buy all kinds of stocks in the stock market.
And I used to buy Revlon and White Rose, Krasdale Foods.
We-- I used to be which-- in that business, so I used to look at the market.
And-- and I started making money.
And I was there for about almost 12 years.
And then, I decided, it's too much work.
I couldn't handle it anymore.
And I had a guy--
Yeah.
And I had a guy working for me.
His name was Louis Moskovitz.
He was a Jewish boy, a very nice boy.
But he couldn't get a job.
He was a tailor.
And his mother used to come and shop in my store.
And he says to me, Harry, you look like a nice Jewish boy.
And-- and I have a son who is also a very nice guy.
He's married.
And he-- he has no job.
He's a tailor.
I says to her, I'll tell you what, Mrs.
Moskovitz, bring in your son, and I'll talk to him.
I'll see what-- If he can help me, I'll give him a job.
He couldn't get a job.
So he-- I-- sure enough, she brought him in the next day.
He was a nice, clean-cut fellow.
And I gave him a job, for Louis.
And he worked with me for about three months.
And his wife came one day to visit him.
He was married.
So his name-- her name was [?
Gitl. ?] I says, [?
Gitl, ?] that's-- I says to Louis, is your wife, [?
Gitl, ?] has a sister?
He says, why?
Because I like-- she looks like a very nice girl.
If she has a sister, maybe we can get together.
I'm looking to, maybe, get married someday.
So sure enough, she says, no, I-- she doesn't have a sister.
But she has a cousin.
And her cousin lives in New Jersey.
And she works in a bakery as a salesgirl.
I says, oh, a cousin is good, too.
[LAUGHS] So he says, why don't you call up your cousin and tell her to come in, over the weekend, to your house, in New York, from New Jersey?
And I'll come over.
And we'll have a cup of coffee and a piece of cake or something.
Sure enough, she came in.
And she came with a boyfriend.
And the boyfriend was also a tailor.
And he-- he didn't have a job.
He collected unemployment, he sure did.
[LAUGHS] And I-- I looked her over.
And she looked like a nice girl.
And-- but I couldn't go out with her, because she was with her boyfriend.
So next day, I called her up on the telephone.
And I says-- her name was Anna.
I says, Ann, would you like to get together with me in New York, just you and me, without your boyfriend?
And we'll go out to a nice, kosher Jewish restaurant and go to a show after we have something to eat?
She says, sure, yeah, I would love that.
So she came in town and to her cousin.
And I went there.
And I picked her up.
And we went out.
And we had a [?
Lustig's. ?] It was a Hungarian-- Hungarian restaurant on East 10th Avenue there, a very nice, kosher restaurant, Hungarian-style cooking.
We had a good meal.
And after the-- after we had the meal, we went to the Radio City Music Hall.
And I took her back to her cousin.
And she went back to New Jersey.
We called each other, every second day or every day, on the telephone.
And a few weeks later, I popped the question to her.
I says, Anna, you want to get married?
Do you want to-- or you want to go out with that schlemiel?
I says, he-- the guy is a [NON-ENGLISH].
He makes [NON-ENGLISH].
I says, how can you make a living from that?
I says, he's a tailor.
He will never get there, I says.
Oh, but you know, she says, oh, but I have nothing.
I says, don't worry.
As long as you see me, you will have everything.
Because I'm a doer, a worker, and an organizer.
And as long as you see me, you will have everything.
Sure enough, we got-- after we went out only three months, we got married.
And I made a beautiful wedding at the Grand Concourse in the Bronx.
It was Rabbi Friedman, a Hungarian Rabbi.
And we had a sit-down dinner for 180 people.
I invited all my customers and my friends and relatives.
And everybody was amazed.
I think this time, it cost me, the wedding, only, I think, $1,500 or $1,600.
Those years were very cheap.
I think $5 or $6 a couple, those years, you know, when we got married in 1943.
And thanks God, we had-- we went on the honeymoon.
And she came back already home from the honeymoon--
--and she had-- she became pregnant on the honeymoon.
We have five children--
--three daughters and two sons.
And we have six grandchildren.
And thanks God, we are quite happy and content now.
Moved to Phoenix in 1961.
I've been working and living in Phoenix.
And I had businesses in Phoenix.
And I almost got killed twice in Phoenix.
I had-- I had a business, which was liquor stores.
You know drive-in liquor stores?
You know, they sell liquor, beer, and wine--
--and magazines.
And once, a guy came in and to-- to rob me.
And he had a gun on me.
He says, I want all your money from the register.
So sure enough, I had in the register only about $80 or $90.
So I gave him the money from the register.
But he wasn't satisfied with that.
So he picked up a big bottle of Korbel champagne and hit me about five, six times in the head and completely busted my skull.
And it bent my brain inside my head.
So the ambulance came.
The fire department came.
And the police came and took me to the hospital.
And I was very badly beaten up.
I was black and blue.
My eyes, my ears, everything was swollen from the damage.
And the doctors couldn't believe this, I'm going to get out of it, because I was brain-damaged.
My brain was bent inside my head.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so, but I-- three or four days later, I went back to work.
Six months later, I got hold up again, and I got shot.
And the bullet went right through me.
A guy shot me.
And that time, I made up my mind, I've got to get out of that business.
And I got out of the liquor business.
And I went in the real estate business.
And I have bought apartments and houses.
And, since then, I'm still in the real estate business.
To my grandchildren?
[CRYING]
[CRYING] I like to tell them this, they should be good citizens.
And-- and-- and-- and they should take-- strive to have a very good education, because the education is a very, very important thing.

And they should try to keep and stay in their Judaism and-- and-- and-- be a-- a-- an example for the other people in the world as what people can do after all this horrible things, what people go through and suffer.
And-- and-- and they should try to be charitable and-- and-- and be nice to-- to all different people, nations, and-- and not to make hatred.
And there should never be hatred in the world, again.
To-- I don't care what race or color or religion you are, that they should try to get along with people and to be nice.
Yeah, thank you.
